text: Both passive and active warm-up can produce temperature, metabolic, neural and psychological effects including increased anaerobic metabolism, elevated oxygen uptake kinetics and post-activation potentiation.
Passive warm-up can increase body temperature without depleting energy substrate stores, unlike active warm-up which involves energy expenditure.
Passive warming techniques can be used during the transition phase between warm-up completion and event start to maintain elevated core and muscle temperature.
Active warm-up induces greater metabolic changes than passive warm-up, leading to increased preparedness for subsequent exercise.
The length of the transition or marshalling phase between warm-up and competition start is a critical practical factor that can diminish warm-up benefits.
Warm-up strategies should be designed specifically for the demands of the individual sport rather than using a generic approach.
